title: Why 6,988 Pages of the Tax Code Are Written to Help You Pay Less
guestBio: Barbara Schreihans is the Founder and CEO of Your Tax Coach, creator of the Write Off Your Life™ Course, and a nationally recognized tax strategist who helps entrepreneurs save tens of millions in taxes while growing their profits.
excerpt: Most entrepreneurs overpay taxes because they don't understand that the tax code is actually a roadmap for paying less, not more. Barbara Schreihans reveals how switching business structures and strategic planning can save you thousands.
metaDescription: Learn why only 12 pages of tax code tell you what to pay while 6,988 pages show you how to pay less. Tax strategy tips for women entrepreneurs.
keyTakeaways: Only 12 pages of the 7,000-page tax code tell you what to pay — the other 6,988 pages show you how to pay less.
S-Corps make you 35 times less likely to be audited than sole proprietorships and can save significant self-employment taxes.
Switching to an S-Corp can save you over $6,000 annually even on just $40,000 of income.
Women have higher investment returns than men but invest less money overall.
Your accountant should explain how to take deductions legally, not just say no to everything.
Strategic December car purchases can result in 100% tax write-offs for business vehicles.
Regular money conversations with your spouse prevent financial conflicts and build wealth together.

pullQuotes.text: The tax code is a roadmap for entrepreneurs, not a punishment
Your accountant should tell you HOW, not just say NO
Women have a HIGHER ROI when they invest — we just need to invest more

timestamps.label: Why taxes feel overwhelming for women
The tax code is a roadmap, not a rulebook
When to start thinking strategically about taxes
Most commonly missed deductions and write-offs
Entity structure: LLC vs S-Corp vs C-Corp
Having money dates with your spouse
Mindset shifts around money and investing

content:
If you've ever felt overwhelmed by taxes or convinced that you're "bad with money," this episode will completely shift your perspective. Aimee sits down with Barbara Schreihans, founder of Your Tax Coach, for a conversation that will change how you see the tax code forever.

Here's the truth that most entrepreneurs don't know: the tax code isn't designed to punish you. In fact, it's quite the opposite. While only 12 pages of the massive 7,000-page tax code tell you what you owe, the remaining 6,988 pages are literally instructions for how to pay less. Think about that for a moment. The government has written you a roadmap for keeping more of your hard-earned money, but most business owners never learn how to read it.

The Structure That's Costing You Thousands

One of the biggest mistakes Barbara sees is entrepreneurs staying in business structures that cost them unnecessary taxes. If you're operating as a sole proprietorship or single-member LLC, you're likely paying 15.3% more in self-employment taxes than you need to. Even on just $40,000 of income, switching to an S-Corp election can save you over $6,000 annually.

But here's what makes this even more compelling: S-Corps make you 35 times less likely to be audited than sole proprietorships. You're not just saving money; you're reducing your audit risk significantly. This isn't about being aggressive or pushing boundaries. This is about using the structures the government has created specifically for business owners.

Barbara shares the story of how she used strategic December car purchasing to write off 100% of a $250,000 vehicle. This wasn't some shady scheme - it was following the tax code exactly as written. The key is understanding that business deductions must have a legitimate business purpose, but that purpose can be much broader than most people realize.

Stop Letting Your Accountant Say No

Too many entrepreneurs have accountants who default to "no" instead of explaining how deductions can work legally. Your tax professional should be your strategic partner, not a roadblock. When you ask about writing off travel, meals, or even that designer handbag you use for client meetings, they should explain the requirements and help you document properly rather than shutting down the conversation.

The reality is that almost everything in your life has some business component if you're an entrepreneur. Your home office, your vehicle, your meals with potential clients, your travel to conferences, even your professional wardrobe - these are legitimate business expenses when documented correctly. The key is understanding the rules and following them precisely.

Building Wealth Beyond Deductions

While write-offs get a lot of attention, Barbara emphasizes that true wealth building comes from strategic tax planning combined with smart investing. This is where women have a massive advantage that most don't realize. Studies consistently show that women achieve higher investment returns than men. We're more methodical, less likely to panic-sell during market downturns, and better at long-term planning.

The problem? Women invest significantly less money overall. We're winning the game we're playing, but we're not playing it at the scale we should be. This often comes down to confidence and starting small. Barbara recommends beginning with just $5 per week if that's what feels manageable. The goal is to build the habit and see your money grow, which builds confidence to invest more.

Retirement accounts offer some of the most powerful wealth-building opportunities for business owners. A Solo 401(k) allows you to contribute as both the employee and employer, potentially allowing you to sock away over $60,000 annually in tax-advantaged accounts. For high earners, cash balance plans can allow even larger contributions while reducing current tax liability.

Money Conversations That Don't End in Fights

Since money is the number one cause of divorce, Barbara advocates for regular "money dates" with your spouse. These aren't confrontational budget meetings, but collaborative sessions where you review goals, celebrate wins, and make decisions together. The key is creating a safe space where both partners can share their concerns and dreams without judgment.

Start by reviewing where you are financially, then discuss where you want to be. Make it enjoyable - order takeout from your favorite restaurant, pour some wine if that's your thing, and approach it as planning your future together rather than solving problems. When both partners are involved in financial decisions, you're much more likely to achieve your goals.

The Mindset Shift That Changes Everything

Perhaps the most important message from this conversation is that you are not bad with money. Women are actually excellent with money when we give ourselves permission to learn and engage with it. The narrative that we're not good at finances is cultural conditioning, not reality.

The tax code was written for business owners and entrepreneurs. Every deduction, every strategic opportunity, every wealth-building vehicle discussed in this episode exists because the government wants to encourage business activity and investment. You're not gaming the system by using these strategies - you're participating in the economy exactly as intended.

Your job as a female entrepreneur is to educate yourself, work with qualified professionals who understand strategy, and stop apologizing for wanting to keep more of your money. The tools are there, the opportunities are real, and you absolutely have what it takes to build the wealth you deserve.

quiz.title: What's Your Money Empowerment Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you approach financial decisions and unlock your unique path to building wealth. Based on insights from episode 45 with tax expert Barbara Schreihans.

questions.question: When you're facing a major financial decision, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Research all the options and create a detailed comparison chart
Trust my gut and move forward if it feels like the right opportunity
Take time to think through all possible outcomes and potential risks
Talk it through with trusted friends or family to get their perspective

questions.question: How do you typically feel about complex financial topics like tax strategy?

options.text: Energized—I love diving into the details and figuring out how everything works
Motivated—if it can help me build wealth, I'm ready to learn it
Cautious—I want to understand it thoroughly before I feel comfortable
Overwhelmed—I prefer when someone can break it down in simple terms

questions.question: When your accountant tells you 'no' to a potential deduction, you:

options.text: Ask for specific documentation about why it doesn't qualify
Push back and ask them to find a legal way to make it work
Accept their expertise but make a mental note to research it later
Trust their judgment—they're the professional, after all

questions.question: What motivates you most about improving your financial situation?

options.text: The satisfaction of optimizing every dollar and maximizing efficiency
Building the freedom and independence to make choices on my own terms
Creating long-term security and stability for the future
Being able to provide for and share experiences with people I care about

questions.question: How do you prefer to learn about new financial strategies?

options.text: Deep-dive articles and detailed case studies with real numbers
Success stories from entrepreneurs who've implemented these strategies
Step-by-step guides that walk through the process methodically
Conversations and explanations that connect the strategy to real life

questions.question: When you think about your current business structure, you feel:

options.text: Like there's probably a more efficient option I should be exploring
Ready to make changes if they'll help me scale and save money
Uncertain about whether what I have now is really the best fit
Confused about the options and wishing someone would just tell me what works

results.title: The Strategic Optimizer
results.description: You have a natural gift for seeing systems and finding ways to make them work better for you. You're not afraid to dive deep into details when it means long-term benefits, and you understand that knowledge is power when it comes to your finances. Your methodical approach means you're perfectly positioned to leverage the tax strategies Barbara shares. This episode will give you concrete tools to optimize your business structure and turn your analytical nature into serious tax savings.

results.title: The Confident Builder
results.description: You trust your instincts and aren't easily intimidated by complex financial topics. You know that taking smart risks and making bold moves is how wealth gets built, and you're ready to advocate for yourself. Your natural confidence is your superpower in financial negotiations and decision-making. Barbara's insights about S-Corp structures and strategic business deductions will resonate with your growth-oriented mindset and give you the ammunition you need to level up your financial game.

results.title: The Thoughtful Planner
results.description: You value stability and prefer to fully understand your options before making moves. You're naturally good at seeing potential risks and want to make sure any financial strategy feels solid and sustainable. Your careful approach protects you from costly mistakes that impulsive decision-makers often face. This episode is perfect for you because Barbara explains exactly how these tax strategies work legally and safely, giving you the confidence to implement changes that align with your thoughtful approach.

results.title: The Relationship Harmonizer
results.description: You understand that money isn't just about numbers—it's about relationships, communication, and shared goals. You naturally see how financial decisions impact everyone around you, and you value transparency and collaboration in money matters. Your strength lies in building consensus and ensuring everyone feels heard in financial discussions. Barbara's emphasis on regular money conversations with spouses and clear communication with tax professionals will speak directly to your collaborative approach to wealth building.

transcript:
**Aimee:** If money is power, then understanding your taxes is a form of leadership. Welcome back to the Manager Podcast, the show where we turn invisible work into visible leadership and help women reclaim their time, power, and potential. I'm your host, Aimee Rickabus. 

Today's conversation is one I wish every entrepreneur could sit in on, because if you've ever felt confused, intimidated, or behind when it comes to taxes, this episode will change the way you see money forever. I'm joined by Barbara Schreihans, founder of yourtaxcoach.com, a nationally recognized tax strategist who helps entrepreneurs and business owners stop overpaying the IRS and start using the tax code as a tool, not a punishment.

I just finished one of Barbara's incredible end of the year Zoom sessions, and I walked away with pages of notes, real usable strategies that immediately shifted how I think about write-offs, entity structure, and long-term wealth planning. This is not about loopholes or cutting corners. This is about education, intention, and empowerment. If you want to move from reacting at tax time to leading your money year round, you're going to love this conversation.

Welcome, Barbara. I am so happy to have you on the show.

**Barbara:** Thank you so much for having me. Yay. Amazing.

**Aimee:** I actually found Barbara on Instagram and saw her work and thought, I have to have her on the show. So I just reached out to her and she graciously said she would come on. I'm so thrilled you're here. Thank you so much. Your work online looks just amazing. And I think the work that you do for women is very empowering and so important for the way we move forward.

**Barbara:** It is so important, especially as women in business and making money. We already have so much on our shoulders. And then as a woman in business, we all have to be smarter with our investments and our decisions with money. So I love sharing the word.

**Aimee:** It's amazing. Yeah. So, Barbara, most women I know dread taxes. Why do you think taxes feel so overwhelming, especially for women business owners?

**Barbara:** I mean, we're not taught about it in school. We have sex ed, but why do we not have tax ed? I say it all the time. And even one of my company's names is tax ed because I'm like, it needs to be talked about. We have and deal with money all day, every day. And taxes are the number one expense in anyone's life.

Studies show that in anyone's lifetime, they will pay 51 percent in taxes and not just income taxes like we all pay. It's property tax. It's sales tax. Every time you are at the checkout counter, there's use tax. There's tariffs. There's so many other things as well. And so I'm like, why not just decrease our number one expense? That's the easiest way to make more money.

**Aimee:** Yes, yes. I've been having this conversation with my husband and he thinks he can, let's just make more money. We'll just make more money. And I'm like, or we could get really smart about it and work on strategies for tax savings. Because I realized how much money we've been paying out in taxes, especially over the last, I'd say the last 10 years of our life. And every year I'm like, okay, we need to get better with tax strategies. We need to get better with tax strategies.

So having my podcast as one of my tools that I use to increase my own knowledge, that has been a big focus on the last few months of my podcasting has been, let's get into money. All the things I'm afraid of, all the things that I feel a little bit weak on, I'm just gonna go out and learn. I'm gonna learn from the best people I can learn from. So I'm just so thrilled you're here.

So you often say that the tax code is actually a roadmap and not a rule book. What do you mean by that?

**Barbara:** Well, why would the government take so much time and energy to write these 7,000 pages if they didn't want you to use it? So really, if you've read the Internal Revenue Code, which you probably haven't, and I hope you haven't, honestly, but I have. And there's only 12 pages that tell you what taxes you need to pay. And the other 6,988 pages tell you how to lower those taxes.

And so it's really there for you. It's for the business owners. It's for the real estate investors. And it's really to stimulate the economy because without business owners and without real estate investors, the government can't hold up the U.S. economy.

**Aimee:** Wow. I love that. I definitely, when I was a real estate professional in my 20s, I was so much better at knowing the rules and there were so many better, it was amazing. I really rarely ever had to pay anything in taxes because there were just so many allowable things, you know, for everything from depreciation to everything improvement that was done on the properties and just all of these expenses that you could write off when you had real estate.

But when we moved into the IT space, I found myself a fish out of water. I didn't know how to avoid the taxes in this vertical, in this model. I didn't know. So this has been a learning process for me. So very glad to have you here.

**Barbara:** Yeah. And real estate's a great way. I've studied economics a lot and economics in multiple different countries, but most countries have the same goals for their government, and that's to have enough food for their people, to have enough housing for their people, and to employ everyone. And if you look at that, that's why the Internal Revenue Code was written.

So if you have a farm, no farmer I've ever met has ever paid anything in taxes, and that's because they need enough food to give to everyone. If you're a business owner and you employ enough employees and you make sure you have a good tax strategist and they're getting all the credits for you, you shouldn't have to pay anything in taxes because they need people employed because they don't want people on government subsidies and they need enough government housing. So that's why they provide it for the landlords and the real estate investors, because they know that they need enough people owning real estate to support the economy.

So when you look at it that way, it's like, that's why there are these incentives. It's not this like, oh, I'm going to be greedy and not pay my fair share. It's no, the government actually needs this stuff.

**Aimee:** What a better perspective. What a beautiful way to look at it and make that mindset shift with taxes. I'm doing work that the government needs me to do. So they're going to reward me with these, you know, basically tax code that rewards this behavior that we're engaging in because it is hard work and it is a risk. We do take risk as business owners. Even as landlords, you take risk.

**Barbara:** Yeah. Yeah. You're willing to do that.

**Aimee:** So at what point should an entrepreneur stop thinking, I'll just ask my CPA at tax time and start thinking strategically about taxes year round?

**Barbara:** Well, I wish more people would do that from the get go and every single year. But unfortunately, us as humans, we need pain in order to change our behavior. So for the most part, it isn't until someone gets a really big tax bill that they were not expecting. And that's when they're like, Oh, maybe I should think about this during the year.

So as a business owner, you really have to think December 31st at midnight, your tax bill starts completely over. So it's as much as you can do until December to lower your taxes. Cause once January 1st happens, which just happened, I don't know when this episode's coming out, but you know, we're recording this at the beginning of the year and it's like everyone's tax bill just started all the way over, which could be a good thing because now we have a long runway. We have 12 months to plan these things.

And actually, my COO and I just met this entire first half of the week of what's our own tax plan for the year? Because we do need the 12 months to start thinking about these things and where to invest and where to put the money and which tax credits do we want to have. And I wish every business owner would do that because there is a way for business owners to pay nothing in tax.

**Aimee:** Yes, totally. But as far as incoming revenue, as soon as you start making, how much money should you look at really taking it seriously?

**Barbara:** Well, it completely depends because we have some clients where maybe one spouse is a really high income W-2 earner. So a lot of their taxes are just taken out every couple of weeks when they get paid, you know? And so the second spouse ends up starting kind of a side business or a hobby, or maybe it's a full-fledged business, but maybe in the beginning, it's just making a couple thousand dollars a year. That is still great. And you should have a tax strategist because we can use that business to have a lot of losses. And those losses will offset your spouse's W-2 income that they already paid taxes on so that next year you get a huge refund.

So to me, I'm always like, I don't care how much your business is making. How much are you paying in total in tax?

**Aimee:** Yes, I love that. That's a beautiful and strategic way to look at it. That, you know, I think from a female perspective, we can all really understand. That's amazing.

So after attending the end of the year session, I realized how many allowable write-offs women simply don't take because they don't know they can. What are some of the most commonly missed deductions that you see?

**Barbara:** Well, one I see missed a lot is just business owners in general being the wrong structure. So a lot of times, I mentioned, maybe you start a business as a side hustle. And so maybe you don't even have an LLC yet, but first step is make sure you have an LLC. That's not going to save you in taxes at all. You'll pay the same amount, but it will protect your butt in case you're ever sued.

But what an LLC can do is it allows us to switch you to an S corporation when it's the right time. And that's so important because if you're not an S corporation, you're paying an additional 15.3% in taxes every single year when you don't have to. You just switched to an S corp and think of if you're just making a hundred grand and you switched to an S corp, you just got 15K back in your pocket. So times it by 200K, 300K. It's a huge tax savings just for how you're structured. So that would be number one.

But then number two, which is what I'm so passionate about as well, is using your business to write off your life. Most of the things in your life could be tied to business if you knew how to tie it and you knew the documentation to support it. And so this could be writing off every single trip you take or every time you go out to eat or a designer handbag or hair and makeup. There's so many ways and things you can tie to business, but you have to know how and when.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. And you know how. So it's all about having the right people around us that can allow us to do those things that we're allowed to do, but we have to do them the right way because you don't want to do them the wrong way. So don't DIY it.

**Barbara:** Yeah. And a lot of people still even they'll be like, oh, well, my accountant said no to that. And I'm like, well, your accountant just doesn't know how and they're afraid to tell you they don't know how. So they just tell you no, instead of actually reading the code and the court cases. And I always say stop letting your accountant say no. There is a way to deduct literally everything. So they should always be telling you how. Maybe it's still a no in the end. Maybe that doesn't apply to you, but there's always a way how.

So if someone wanted to deduct clothing, most accountants will say no. And it's like, no, there is a way. Maybe that's not the way you want to do it and it doesn't apply to you. So then you don't. But there is still a way.

**Aimee:** I love it. Yeah. Tell me about you. I know you were talking recently about how you're going to get a new, you got a new car at the end of this, at the end of 2025, because you know, your car was about 10 years old. So it's time to get a new one. So instead of waiting until January 1st or 2nd to buy your car, you want to go ahead and tell us your story.

**Barbara:** I think the best time to buy a car is in December because the car deduction is based on the percentage that you use it business versus personal. And most of us out there, most people don't have just a personal car and just a business car. Most of us have a car that we use for both. And you really want the percentage of business use to be as high as you can. So that way you get the biggest deduction.

So if you buy a car in December, then as much as you can, only drive it for business in December and you could still get the entire write-off for that year. So 2025, I bought a new car mid-December. I only drove it for business between mid-December and December 31st. So I got 100% deduction on a $250,000 car.

**Aimee:** Yes. Because it's over 6,000 pounds. That's the car, right?

**Barbara:** Yes. Yeah. So it has to be over 6,000 pounds. But not the actual weight of the car. It's super weird. It's called GVWR. So when you are going to a car dealership, you can Google now the VIN number of the car and put in GVWR and see what it is. If it's over 6,000 pounds, then it counts. But a lot of times it'll be the actual weight of the car is 4,500, but the GVWR is 6,200. So it does count.

But a lot of times people are looking at the wrong numbers or the opposite. The salesperson at the car dealership a lot of times will tell you that a car qualifies. And let me tell you, they're not a tax professional. So don't just take their advice. I learned this almost the hard way. I was at a Porsche dealership two years ago. I typically buy a car every year because we already do all the tax strategies that we want to and we invest a ton. And I just like loud and fast cars.

So that the Porsche dealership and I was going to buy a car at the end of the year. And he's like, yeah, the Porsche Macan qualifies. And I almost trusted him. And then at the very end, I Googled. And the 2025 Porsche Macan was 5,990 pounds. But the 24 was over 6,000 pounds. They changed the weight of the car. And I was like, that was kind of dumb from a business perspective, knowing that businesses want 6,000 pound cars. Right. So that was kind of silly of Porsche.

But then Tesla specifically, Elon specifically, and he's said this in interviews, built a car that was 6,001 pounds just to be like, F you to the IRS. I love it when people know how to play the game. Elon's really good at playing the game.

**Aimee:** He definitely knows how to play the game. That is amazing. For me, one of the biggest things that I didn't know about was the inventory to expenses column on companies that are under 25 million because we have another company that is under $25 million that does hardware, computer hardware. And we do carry a massive amount of inventory. And I looked at my husband after I watched your Zoom, and I said, guess what, babe? That whole inventory, we can move to the expense column. And he goes, seriously? I'm like, yeah, since 2018, now I can quote the tax law on it, right?

**Barbara:** Yes. Yeah, thanks for that. On your taxes, it's a change of accounting method. So we'll just need to make sure your accountant's doing that right. But on the P&L, right? I mean, how cool that you went to a free masterclass and you got a million dollar deduction.

**Aimee:** That's what I thought. I was like, okay, get the value. I'm going to use value in my promos. Seriously. Seriously. I thought, okay, well, I'll learn something. I always learn something. But, you know, sometimes you can learn a little something that can save you a whole lot of money.

But we as women love to learn things. And so I think that as we expand ourselves, as we kind of delve into the things that we're afraid of or that we feel like we're not that good at, as we kind of intentionally make the choices to surround ourselves with the content that will help us to fill those knowledge gaps, I mean, I feel like women are going to be totally unstoppable in the information age.

**Barbara:** Yes, I can go on and on about women. Statistics show women make more money when they invest it. The problem is we don't invest as much as men. But when we do, we have a higher ROI. And female-backed VCs also perform better. And there's just so many reasons why women need to be in business. I'm so excited. That's my whole, the Apple box I stand on is, yeah, let's go, girls.

**Aimee:** We've got this. We manage things. We've been managing the households for millennia. And it's nice to see us be able to get an ROI on the invisible labor of running our households for all those, you know, all of that DNA that went into us that helped us to become incredible multitaskers and incredible managers with very high EQ and to take that and to be able to use that in the real world outside of our homes is definitely something that I'm super passionate about.

Back to taxes. Can you explain the difference between a write-off and a true tax strategy? Because they're not the same thing, right?

**Barbara:** No, not really. A write-off is something that you can deduct in your business. So somehow you pay for something and then you tie it to your business, you get to deduct it. So a car, for example, you buy a $100,000 car, you can deduct it in your business, but you're only saving your tax rate. So if you're in a 40% tax bracket on a $100,000 car, you're saving $40,000 in tax, but you're still out $60,000. I think that's what people really need to understand is a deduction doesn't just mean it's free. It just means you're getting it at a discount and the discount is your tax rate. We still like discounts and I like getting loud and fast cars at discounts. I do.

But then there are strategies and those are it sounds more strategic than just go out and buy something. So, you know, we will tell our clients if they've done all of the strategies we've told them, yeah, sure. If you need a new car, go buy a new car or you need a new computer or a new camera or, you know, whatever. Go for that.

But really, the strategies to me are things that make you money. Write-offs cost you money at a discount. And strategies should increase your net worth. So a lot of times our strategies that we use with our clients, they're investments that can grow their net worth or it's shifting income around. So it's at a lower tax bracket. So they're getting more money for free or switching it to tax free income, which is the best type of income. Or it could be tax credits, which are way better than a tax deduction because they're dollar for dollar money in your bank account. So they're just more high level things that you can do as a business owner. Typically, we can do some strategies for W-2 earners, but it's so much easier when you own a business.

**Aimee:** That's amazing. And it's one of those things, you know, as we become better business women, we get so passionate about what we do. You know, we're so passionate about IT. We're so passionate about, my husband knows a ton about networking hardware and, you know, that's his world. But we also, as we're growing our businesses, if we want to be really good business people, we also need to focus on these things like tax strategies so that we can retain the money for the company that the company has made. Because the lifeblood of the company is the capital behind the company. And if we're giving the capital back to the government, we're not able to grow the company into the robust company it really needs to be. You know, so that's another way to look at this.

**Barbara:** And I've seen companies a $2 million company versus a $10 million company. I've seen it where the $2 million company actually takes home more money than the $10 million company because they had no tax plan. One that has to do with profitability, of course, but then also just the tax rates, you could make less than a $2 million company. So imagine if they paid nothing in tax, they would have millions more.

**Aimee:** Exactly. Yeah. And it's strategy is everything. So when does entity structure really matter? How do women know when it's time to move beyond that sole prop or the single member LLC?

**Barbara:** I was just having this conversation. I really think from the very beginning of business, you should really think about it. Cause also if you're starting a business, if you think it's going to fail from the beginning, it's probably going to fail. So why not start it and be like, this thing is going to be successful. And I know I'm going to need to be an S corporation. So why not be an S corporation from the beginning?

So one, I think it's a mindset thing, but also even in the beginning, let's say you make 40 grand your first year, just by switching to an S corp, you're saving $6,000. That's not nothing. That's a great family vacation somewhere or a week at Disney. And $6,000 to someone that's only making 40K means a whole heck of a lot. So stop letting your accountants tell you you're not ready yet. Oh, you need to be at 100,000, 200,000. I hear it all the time from other people's accountants. I'm like, you're costing your clients money.

And when you're not a sole prop, well, I'll reword it. When you're an S corporation, you're 35 times less likely to be audited than when you're a sole prop or single member LLC. So even if you're making no money, to me, I'm like, is it worth being 35 times less likely to be audited? Yes. Because just the time and money and expense and paying an attorney, a tax attorney, or a CPA to go to tax court for you is going to cost you tens of thousands of dollars. So just be an S-corp from the beginning. Or a C-corp. Yeah. Interesting. Well, maybe not a C. I think S is the best.

**Aimee:** We did the C-Corp thing for a while and it was very expensive.

**Barbara:** C-Corps are really great in a few scenarios. And I do have a C-Corp. One is to change the timing on your income. So if you ever have a big year and you want to shift income to not pay taxes on it for another two years, C-Corps are really good. I love that strategy. We do that for a lot of clients just to give them more runway to buy investment properties or something to lower their taxes.

They're really great if you're going to sell your company later because of the qualified small business stock election. So if you are a company that qualifies, you could exclude up to $15 million in gain, which is huge and per shareholder. So even more huge.

They're really great if you want to donate a lot of money or if you just know that you don't need all the money out of your business. So we have one client who is so successful. She makes $7 million and she needs 200K to live off of. So she's very simple and that's all she needs. And the rest just stays in her C-Corp and she doesn't get taxed on it because she doesn't take it out. So there are instances where C-Corps make sense.

**Aimee:** Interesting. Nice. And is there any downside of an S-Corp? Because I was having this convo with a friend of mine who's in operations and she was saying how she really wants to stay in LLC and not become an S-Corp, what's the downside of becoming an S-Corp? Is there any?

**Barbara:** Maybe, I don't think it's a downside, but just more admin stuff. You need a separate tax return now. Because S-Corps are a separate return, whereas a single member LLC is just on your personal. But again, you're way more likely to be audited when it's on your personal. So is the admin work worth it? And you're going to pay someone else to do the return anyway. So whatever.

And you do have to pay a payroll company. Because now you have to be on payroll. But we use Gusto. It's $59 a month. I mean, those are the hiccups. But I wouldn't say they're downsides. There's really no downside.

**Aimee:** Nice. Well, that's good to know. I love talking through the things, you know, especially because these are conversations I'm so glad that women are starting to have with each other.

**Barbara:** Yeah. Yes.

**Aimee:** So tell me, what's one tax move you wish women would make before they cross into higher income tax brackets, not after?

**Barbara:** Using really big retirement accounts, because I think a lot of us rely on the men in our life to handle the finances, to deal with the investments. And we all need to think about retiring now and retirement now, not later, because of the time value of money. And when you're a business owner, why not have it be a business deduction?

So there's really great retirement accounts just depending on where you're at in business. If you don't have employees, a solo 401k is so good. You can put $72,000 in it this year and it's all a business deduction. If you have employees, then a safe harbor 401k with profit sharing. And then you could even add on cash balance plans or defined benefit plan where you can put up to 400K a person into. And so now you're getting hundreds of thousands of dollars in deductions in your business, but that money is just going into your own retirement account and making you money instead of the IRS.

**Aimee:** Amazing. That's one of the tax strategies I need to adopt in 2026 for sure. Yeah. Yeah. Definitely. We have not, I think my husband and I both as entrepreneurs have always been like, oh, we'll just, when we're ready to retire, we'll just sell the companies, you know? And we, so we really haven't been doing the whole saving for your retirement thing in the traditional sense, but it is such a great tax strategy to do it that way.

**Barbara:** It's a tax strategy. It grows your net worth. And if you offer it to employees and it's a really great retention tool as well because it makes them very sticky.

**Aimee:** No, I like it. I think that is how we can show up, you know, and bring make the visible work visible when it comes to making sure that we're contributing to because taxes are the biggest thing that we have. It's our biggest expense in our lifetime. We as women need to be more proactive about it and saying, hey, let's let's do this better. Let's save some money for our family.

If there's a way we can, my husband and I do, and I, I tell people to do this all the time, is to have a monthly money date. And I will say when you first start having it, at least for us, our experience, it was very we would get in these little fights when we'd have these money dates because we each approach money and investing differently and have different mindsets. But if you don't talk about it, you're not going to work through those things. And when money is the number one reason for divorce, you have to work through it.
